skill.name: shellcheck-configuration
skill.description: Configure and use ShellCheck to improve shell script quality, portability, and CI linting workflows.
skill.summary: Guides ShellCheck setup, rule configuration, suppressions, and CI integration for shell scripts.
skill.author: sickn33

security_audit.summary: Static analysis reported many high and medium patterns, but review found they are documentation examples for ShellCheck usage, configuration, and CI integration. No prompt injection, hidden automation, credential access, data exfiltration, or malicious execution intent was found in SKILL.md.

low_findings.title: Privileged Package Installation Example Is Documentation-Only
low_findings.description: The sudo command appears inside a GitHub Actions example for installing ShellCheck. It is not hidden automation and does not execute when the skill is loaded, but users should review privileged commands before reuse.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The command is contained in a fenced CI workflow example and directly supports the ShellCheck setup topic. No surrounding text instructs the assistant to run it automatically or conceal the action.

low_findings.title: External Command Examples Are Expected ShellCheck Guidance
low_findings.description: Commands such as shellcheck, find, git diff, xargs, and sha256sum are instructional examples for linting shell scripts. They are not embedded scripts or executable skill hooks.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The commands are fenced examples that match the declared purpose of configuring ShellCheck. There is no user-input command construction, persistence mechanism, or automatic execution path.

low_findings.title: Filesystem References Are ShellCheck Configuration Examples
low_findings.description: Hidden-file references point to .shellcheckrc and cache examples used for ShellCheck configuration. They do not access secrets or instruct collection of private files.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The dotfile and cache paths are ordinary configuration examples for this tool category. No evidence found of credential harvesting, destructive filesystem operations, or hidden persistence.

low_findings.title: Network URLs Are Reference Links and Source Install Instructions
low_findings.description: The hardcoded URLs point to the official ShellCheck repository and public documentation. No code sends local data to those URLs.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The URLs are visible documentation links and one git clone example for the ShellCheck project. I did not find evidence of outbound data transfer or suspicious domains.

low_findings.title: Security Keyword Matches Are ShellCheck Rule Content
low_findings.description: C2 and reconnaissance keyword alerts map to ordinary ShellCheck rule examples, process-listing examples, or output text. They do not show command-and-control behavior.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The matched text appears in educational examples of ShellCheck warnings and common shell patterns. No evidence found of beaconing, remote control, credential staging, or evasion instructions.

low_findings.title: Weak Cryptography Alerts Lack Supporting Evidence
low_findings.description: The static weak-cryptography alerts occur on descriptive ShellCheck text rather than cryptographic operations. No weak algorithm use was found at the reported lines.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited lines describe ShellCheck and its warning categories, not cryptographic code. The only hashing example reviewed is for cache keys, not security-sensitive cryptography.

dangerous_patterns.title: Fenced Shell Command Examples
dangerous_patterns.description: The skill contains many shell snippets for ShellCheck installation, linting, pre-commit hooks, and CI. These are expected for the topic and should be treated as user-reviewed examples.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The examples are visible, topic-relevant, and not connected to a runnable skill script. Their risk is operational misuse if copied without review, not malicious marketplace content.

dangerous_patterns.title: ShellCheck Suppression and Source Examples
dangerous_patterns.description: The skill demonstrates ShellCheck suppressions and sourced-file hints. This can hide warnings if overused, but the text frames suppressions as specific and documented.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: Suppressions are a legitimate ShellCheck feature, but broad disable directives can reduce lint coverage. The skill also advises documenting exclusions and addressing violations, which lowers the risk.

content.value_statement: Shell scripts often fail because portability and quoting problems are hard to spot during review. This skill guides ShellCheck setup, rule tuning, suppressions, and CI workflows for safer script maintenance.
actual_capabilities: Explains when to use ShellCheck for Bash, POSIX sh, dash, and related shell scripts.
Shows common ShellCheck configuration options for project-level and environment-level settings.
Guides CI, GitHub Actions, GitLab CI, and pre-commit integration patterns.
Describes common ShellCheck warning families and practical remediation patterns.
Covers suppression comments and configuration exclusions with review guidance.
Provides examples for output formats, parallel checking, and cache-based lint workflows.
limitations: It is guidance only and does not include executable automation files.
It does not replace manual review of shell scripts or CI permissions.
Some examples must be adapted for each project shell, platform, and package manager.
It focuses on ShellCheck and does not cover every shell security scanner.

use_cases.title: Set Up Repository Shell Linting
use_cases.description: Add ShellCheck configuration and CI checks that match a repository shell dialect and quality requirements.

use_cases.title: Fix Script Quality Issues
use_cases.description: Interpret ShellCheck warnings and apply safer quoting, control-flow, and portability patterns.

use_cases.title: Standardize Team Rules
use_cases.description: Create documented exclusions, output formats, and review expectations for shell script contributions.

prompt_templates.title: Choose a Basic Configuration
prompt_templates.prompt: Help me choose a ShellCheck configuration for a repository that uses Bash scripts. Explain the key options and what I should verify before committing it.

prompt_templates.title: Review ShellCheck Warnings
prompt_templates.scenario: Fixing lint output from existing scripts
prompt_templates.prompt: Review these ShellCheck warnings and group them by severity, likely cause, and recommended fix. Note any warnings that may need project-specific judgment.

prompt_templates.title: Design CI Enforcement
prompt_templates.scenario: Adding quality gates to pull requests
prompt_templates.prompt: Design a ShellCheck CI workflow for my repository. Include target shell selection, failure behavior, output format, and how to handle justified suppressions.

prompt_templates.title: Create a Team Policy
prompt_templates.prompt: Create a ShellCheck adoption policy for multiple teams. Cover shared defaults, exceptions, review rules, rollout stages, and metrics for reducing recurring warnings.

output_examples.input: A team wants ShellCheck added to pull requests for Bash scripts.
output_examples.output: A concise CI plan with target shell settings, failure conditions, review guidance for suppressions, and a checklist for validating the workflow.

output_examples.input: A developer receives several ShellCheck warnings on a deployment script.
output_examples.output: A prioritized explanation of each warning category, recommended fixes, and notes about changes that may alter runtime behavior.

output_examples.input: An engineering lead wants consistent ShellCheck rules across projects.
output_examples.output: A team policy outline with shared defaults, allowed exceptions, documentation requirements, and rollout steps for existing repositories.

best_practices: Select the target shell explicitly before interpreting ShellCheck results.
Document every suppression with the reason and the expected review owner.
Run ShellCheck locally and in CI so issues are caught before merge.
anti_patterns: Disabling broad warning groups without recording why they are safe.
Analyzing Bash scripts as POSIX sh or POSIX scripts as Bash.
Copying privileged installation commands into CI without reviewing permissions.

faq.question: Does this skill run ShellCheck automatically?
faq.answer: No. It provides guidance and examples. Users or agents must decide when to run commands.

faq.question: Can it help with Bash and POSIX shell scripts?
faq.answer: Yes. It explains target shell selection and configuration for Bash, sh, dash, ksh, and similar shells.

faq.question: Does it include CI examples?
faq.answer: Yes. It covers GitHub Actions, GitLab CI, and pre-commit patterns for ShellCheck enforcement.

faq.question: Can ShellCheck warnings be suppressed?
faq.answer: Yes. The skill explains suppression patterns and recommends documenting why each exception is acceptable.

faq.question: Is this a security scanning skill?
faq.answer: It focuses on shell static analysis quality. It can catch risky shell patterns but is not a full security scanner.

faq.question: What should I review before using the examples?
faq.answer: Review package installation commands, CI permissions, target shell settings, and any broad warning exclusions.
